name: dueling sword
shortDescription: A slim dueling sword balanced for quick footwork and clean wrist turns, more at home in a courtyard dispute than a battlefield crush. Its value depends on straightness, grip wear, and whether the last owner was known for winning arguments the expensive way.
flavorText: “A duel is a conversation where the punctuation is steel.”

name: starcourt broadsword
shortDescription: A broad courtly sword with polished fittings and star-shaped ornament worked into the guard. Collectors prize the ceremony in it, while practical buyers still test the edge, because noble decoration does not excuse poor tempering.
flavorText: “Starcourt blades shine first for witnesses and second for enemies.”

name: falchion
shortDescription: A curved single-edged sword with enough forward weight to make every swing feel decisive. Traders move falchions quickly in frontier towns, where guards, caravan hands, and boastful recruits all like a blade that looks ready before it is even drawn.
flavorText: “It is not subtle, but neither are most road problems.”

name: falchion of revenge
shortDescription: A grim curved blade whose name does half the bargaining before the steel leaves its wrapping. Its surface shows careful maintenance rather than display polish, suggesting an owner who cared more about purpose than parade.
flavorText: “Revenge is a poor business plan, but it sells remarkably well.”

name: fancy claymore
shortDescription: A large two-handed claymore dressed with elegant grip work and bright metal accents. It is too imposing for casual buyers, but wealthy captains and hall-keepers admire the mix of battlefield reach and ceremonial grandeur.
flavorText: “Some swords guard a doorway simply by being noticed.”

name: fancy flat falchion
shortDescription: A flattened falchion with decorative fittings, made to look refined without losing the blade’s heavy chopping character. It suits buyers who want the menace of a road weapon and the manners of something suitable for a noble rack.
flavorText: “Court polish over caravan teeth.”

name: fabled greatsword of wealth
shortDescription: A legendary greatsword associated with treasure vaults, paid armies, and patrons who believed gold could be sharpened into authority. Even wrapped in plain cloth, it draws the sort of buyer who asks about provenance before asking about price.
flavorText: “It was said to cut debt, loyalty, and occasionally armor.”

name: fancy long cutlass
shortDescription: A long cutlass with refined hardware and a confident curve, favored by sea captains who want more reach without giving up close-deck handling. Its scabbard matters almost as much as the blade, since half its value is swagger.
flavorText: “A shipboard blade that learned to bow before drawing blood.”

name: fancy short sword
shortDescription: A compact sword upgraded with careful grip binding, clean fittings, and just enough decoration to look respectable at a noble belt. It sells well to merchants who want protection that does not announce panic.
flavorText: “Small enough for discretion, polished enough for dinner.”

name: fabled sword of flaming death
shortDescription: A fearsome named sword surrounded by stories of heat, omens, and battles that ended with witnesses swearing the air itself had caught fire. Whether its enchantment still wakes or merely sleeps, no cautious merchant stores it beside dry ledgers.
flavorText: “The receipt should include a bucket.”

name: flat falchion
shortDescription: A broad, flattened falchion with a workmanlike edge and plain construction. It lacks romance, but practical guards appreciate weapons that price honestly, sharpen easily, and do not demand a heroic backstory.
flavorText: “A plain curve can still settle a crooked road.”

name: flat longsword
shortDescription: A straight longsword with a wide flat blade and simple lines, built for steady service rather than admiration. Buyers judge it by balance, edge, and whether the fuller is clean, not by songs attached to the hilt.
flavorText: “It has no legend, which means nobody has overcharged for one yet.”

name: fabled solid gold sword
shortDescription: A legendary sword of solid gold, too impractical for common combat and too magnificent for common sense. Nobles, temples, and vault keepers compete for it as a symbol of power, knowing perfectly well that the metal is softer than the rumors.
flavorText: “It cuts pride more reliably than armor.”

name: greatsword
shortDescription: A heavy two-handed sword built for reach, commitment, and owners with shoulders worth trusting. It takes space to display and more space to swing, so caravan merchants usually sell it to guards who already know what they are carrying.
flavorText: “A greatsword is a promise made with both hands.”

name: greatsword of eagles
shortDescription: A proud greatsword marked with eagle motifs along the guard and fuller, giving it the air of high banners and mountain campaigns. It appeals to commanders, collectors, and anyone who wants their weapon to suggest clear skies and high claims.
flavorText: “It was made for people who look up before charging.”

name: greatsword of honor
shortDescription: A solemn greatsword whose clean lines and careful finish make it feel more like a vow than a tool. Buyers often ask who carried it, because a blade named for honor gathers value from conduct as much as craftsmanship.
flavorText: “A blade can remember a promise longer than its owner can.”

name: notable katana
shortDescription: A notable katana with a calm curve, tight wrapping, and a surface that rewards close inspection. It is not merely exotic trade stock; the right buyer studies the temper line before ever discussing coin.
flavorText: “Some blades are appraised in silence first.”

name: fabled katana of Goldreef
shortDescription: A fabled katana tied to Goldreef tales, reef lords, and treasure carried inland under guard. Its curve catches light with unnerving ease, making it a prize for collectors who prefer their legends sharp and difficult to insure.
flavorText: “Goldreef sent pearls, salt, and one sword nobody could forget.”

name: scarce katana of stealth
shortDescription: A scarce katana with dark fittings and a reputation for disappearing into shadowed inventories. Thieves admire it, guards mistrust it, and honest merchants keep records twice when it passes through their hands.
flavorText: “The finest stealth blade is the one everyone denies buying.”

name: long cutlass
shortDescription: A long cutlass made for reach on rolling decks and crowded docks, where straight lines are rare and quick turns matter. Its curved edge carries the practical swagger of sailors, smugglers, and dock captains with unpaid enemies.
flavorText: “Long enough for respect, curved enough for trouble.”

name: longsword
shortDescription: A dependable longsword of familiar shape, useful to soldiers, guards, and travelers who want a blade without explaining it. Its price rises or falls on balance, steel quality, and whether the hilt has survived honest use.
flavorText: “The common blade survives because common dangers do.”

name: large longsword
shortDescription: A larger longsword with extra presence in the blade and a grip that expects stronger hands. It is favored by gate guards and broad-shouldered adventurers who want reach without stepping fully into greatsword territory.
flavorText: “Not every large problem needs two hands, but it helps.”

name: longsword of fate
shortDescription: A named longsword that carries more superstition than ornament, often bought by people standing at the edge of some grand decision. Sensible merchants price the steel; ambitious buyers pay extra for the feeling that the blade already knows tomorrow.
flavorText: “Fate charges no coin, but its equipment is expensive.”

name: enchanted longsword of punishing
shortDescription: An enchanted longsword with a stern reputation, said to bite hardest when wielded in judgment rather than anger. Buyers ask careful questions about command words, residual magic, and whether the sword has opinions of its own.
flavorText: “Punishment should never be left in a damp scabbard.”

name: royal longsword of Highbell
shortDescription: A royal longsword associated with Highbell courts, bright processions, and guards trained to stand perfectly still. Its crest work makes provenance important, and a forged history can halve the price faster than a nicked edge.
flavorText: “Highbell blades ring loudest in rooms where nobody dares draw them.”

name: ironclan great sword
shortDescription: A massive Ironclan great sword built with uncompromising weight and clan-stamped fittings. It appeals to warriors who distrust delicate steel and collectors who understand that some cultures sign their work in mass.
flavorText: “Ironclan smiths do not whisper through metal.”

name: ironclan long sword
shortDescription: An Ironclan long sword with stout construction, plain authority, and a hilt made for hard use rather than soft gloves. It is less glamorous than court steel, but caravans like weapons that survive weather, impact, and bad decisions.
flavorText: “Clan steel prefers function, then reputation, then decoration if time remains.”

name: rapier
shortDescription: A narrow rapier built for point work, court confidence, and owners who believe distance can be measured in etiquette. It sells best in cities, where a quick wrist and a sharp insult often travel together.
flavorText: “A rapier makes even silence look prepared.”

name: royal rapier
shortDescription: A royal rapier with refined guard work and the delicate menace of high court fashion. Its blade is slender, its manners expensive, and its buyer usually cares as much about being seen wearing it as surviving with it.
flavorText: “Royal steel pricks deeper when everyone is watching.”

name: sabre
shortDescription: A curved sabre with a quick military feel, suited to riders, scouts, and officers who prefer speed over ceremony. The best examples carry scuff marks from saddle and road rather than from dusty display hooks.
flavorText: “A sabre likes open ground and impatient horses.”

name: scimitar
shortDescription: A graceful scimitar with a strong curve and a bright cutting edge, valued in markets where beauty and practicality are allowed to share a scabbard. Traders check the spine carefully, since elegance can hide careless forging.
flavorText: “A curve can be a warning written beautifully.”

name: scimitar of lies
shortDescription: A named scimitar with a reputation for false oaths, disguised bargains, and owners who never told the same story twice. Buyers interested in it usually ask whether the blade lies, or whether it simply attracts liars.
flavorText: “The edge was honest. The hands around it rarely were.”

name: scimitar of wealth
shortDescription: A richly associated scimitar whose polished curve suggests merchant princes, guarded caravans, and disputes settled before ledgers close. It is a status blade first, but the steel still carries enough authority to keep negotiations polite.
flavorText: “Profit prefers a curved signature.”

name: short sword
shortDescription: A compact fighting sword useful in alleys, tight halls, and caravan wagons where longer blades become furniture. It is one of the easiest weapons to sell because nearly every buyer can imagine needing it tomorrow.
flavorText: “Short steel is what long trouble becomes indoors.”

name: short sword of blindness
shortDescription: A named short sword linked to sudden flashes, darkened vision, and tavern stories told by survivors with uncertain memories. Even if the magic has faded, cautious traders keep its point covered and its history written clearly.
flavorText: “Some blades win by being the last thing clearly seen.”

name: short sword with gem
shortDescription: A short sword set with a decorative gem in the hilt, making it part weapon and part vanity purchase. Its value depends on whether the stone is genuine, securely mounted, and not worth more than the blade around it.
flavorText: “A gem in the grip does not improve courage, but buyers keep hoping.”

name: scarce silver sabre
shortDescription: A scarce silver sabre prized by collectors, monster hunters, and nobles who like their superstition polished. It requires careful storage to preserve both edge and shine, but the right rumor can double its price overnight.
flavorText: “Silver makes a blade expensive before it proves useful.”

name: fabled singing sword of gold
shortDescription: A fabled golden sword said to hum, sing, or complain depending on the hand that holds it. Bards consider it priceless, soldiers consider it distracting, and merchants consider it impossible to display quietly.
flavorText: “It knew three ballads and one very rude marching song.”

name: small falchion
shortDescription: A smaller falchion with a stout curve and easier handling than its heavier cousins. It suits travelers who want a chopping edge without carrying a weapon that catches on every doorway and wagon rail.
flavorText: “A little curve can still make a large argument.”

name: small royal falchion
shortDescription: A compact royal falchion with decorative fittings and a surprisingly practical bite. It is a favorite among court guards, lesser nobles, and collectors who want regal style without hanging a full parade weapon from their belt.
flavorText: “Royal does not always mean large. Sometimes it means expensive.”

name: strong bardiche
shortDescription: A strong bardiche with a broad cutting head and a haft meant to take punishment. It is awkward in cramped shops but impressive in a guard yard, where buyers can imagine it holding a gate line with stubborn authority.
flavorText: “A bardiche is what a door hires when hinges lose confidence.”

name: strong falchion
shortDescription: A reinforced falchion with extra confidence in the spine and enough weight to feel honest in the hand. It lacks delicate beauty, but sells well wherever roads are rough and repair smiths are far apart.
flavorText: “Strong steel forgives bad weather better than bad judgment.”

name: sword of cerebov
shortDescription: A named sword tied to Cerebov tales, old border quarrels, and collectors who argue over maps while holding the blade. Its true origin may be disputed, but the workmanship is real enough to keep bids alive.
flavorText: “Nobody agrees where Cerebov ended, but everyone agrees the sword traveled.”

name: fabled sword of dragon tongues
shortDescription: A fabled sword associated with dragon speech, oath flames, and negotiations that no sane interpreter would accept. Scholars want it for legends, warriors want it for prestige, and merchants want it stored far from sleeping wyrm cultists.
flavorText: “It was said to understand dragons. Sensibly, it rarely answered them.”

name: fabled sword of legends
shortDescription: A fabled sword whose name is almost too large for any single history to support. Every buyer brings a favorite tale to attach to it, and a patient merchant lets each tale raise the price a little before speaking.
flavorText: “Legends grow fastest around polished steel.”

name: sword of calm power
shortDescription: A named sword with a quiet presence, valued by duelists and commanders who distrust flashy enchantments. It feels less like a weapon eager to act and more like one waiting for the exact moment action becomes necessary.
flavorText: “Power that does not hurry is harder to bargain with.”

name: fabled sword of powerful grip
shortDescription: A fabled sword famous for a grip that seems to settle into the hand with unnatural certainty. Buyers test it nervously, because a blade that refuses to slip may also be slow to let go.
flavorText: “The handle remembered confidence even when the wielder forgot.”

name: triple sword of the sun
shortDescription: A strange triple sword bearing solar motifs and the kind of construction that makes armorers argue in public. It is rare, theatrical, and difficult to price, which means collectors arrive smiling and accountants arrive frowning.
flavorText: “Three edges, one sun, and several questions from the guild inspector.”

name: fabled sword of vibration
shortDescription: A fabled sword said to tremble with stored force, singing softly against its scabbard when the room grows tense. Even dormant, it unsettles shelves, glassware, and anyone trying to appraise it with steady hands.
flavorText: “It hummed before trouble, which made trouble feel punctual.”

name: fabled sword of Stormmere
shortDescription: A fabled sword tied to Stormmere winds, black water, and old tales of blades drawn beneath thunderclouds. Its value sits between relic and warning, the sort of item buyers approach as if the weather might follow it indoors.
flavorText: “Stormmere keeps its rain in the sky and its promises in steel.”
